comments.text: I have posted a short write-up and verification package concerning this problem: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21518 The note does not prove infinitude, but it gives two further primary pseudoperfect numbers, with verification. The first is 5998279018951962402 = 2·3·11·17·101·157·1979·10093·16879, satisfying 1/n + sum_{p|n} 1/p = 1. Moreover n+1 = 5998279018951962403 is prime, so this gives another example by the usual inheritance construction: 35979351189199316534587473905773572006 = n(n+1). The write-up includes the integer identities, a Pocklington certificate for n+1, and a port-filling framework which explains how these examples arise from the port (113322,797). I regard this as a partial result toward the problem, not a solution of the infinitude question.

This problem has a solution of the form A0=m1= 6, p1=2,p2=3 ; 1/2+1/3=1-1:6 a2=42= 2.3.(6+1) =2.3.7 : 1/2+1/3+1/7=1-1/42 a3= 1806= 2.3.(6+1).(42+1) ; 1/2+1/3+1/7+1/43=1-1/1806 This sequence continues infinitely and these form solutions
Unfortunately the very next instance of this pattern, $1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + 1/43 + 1/1807 = 1 - 1/3263442$, does not work, because $1807 = 13 \times 139$ is not prime.
